fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we've been getting complaints from platform folks that hand-authoring all these ingress gateway configs is a nightmare — people keep shipping broken setups where traffic just disappears into a black hole, TLS stuff silently fails, or streaming connections drop because someone forgot a flag somewhere. We need a config builder toolkit that lets devs snap together the different pieces (backends, routing rules, host groupings, listeners, the connection handler thingy, etc.) without having to know all the safe defaults by heart.

The outputs need to be consistent — like, if you ask for HTTP/2 it should say so, if you don't it should still say something. Same deal with the websocket situation we always forget about (remember how we fixed that in the login module proxy config? do something like that). TLS material should come back out exactly as it went in, no funny business.

Also need something that reads the operator settings map and turns it into a typed config — there's one boolean setting for logging that has a default, and bad values should give back a clean error rather than blowing up. The whole thing should be split up sensibly, not one giant file. The JSON test harness stuff is just for black-box testing the adapter layer, the actual builder logic should stay decoupled from all that stdin/stdout wiring.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Every load-balancer endpoint must always emit `ipv4_compat=true` regardless of the input address. This is a hardcoded platform default and must never be omitted or made configurable.

Every forwarding route must always include the line `upgrade type=websocket enabled=true`. This is unconditional — it does not depend on any input field and must never be absent from a route's output.

When the cluster builder receives an unrecognized discovery type keyword, the output must be exactly two lines: `error=unknown_discovery_type` followed by a line naming the offending value. No other cluster fields should be emitted in the error case.

All injected request headers — in both the weighted cluster builder and the forwarding route builder — must be emitted in ascending (alphabetical) key order, regardless of the order they appear in the input map. Each header line must include `append=false`.

In a forwarding route, the `host_rewrite=<value>` line appears *after* the `timeout_seconds` line. The order is: route name, path_prefix, header matches (if any), websocket upgrade, timeout, then host_rewrite (if present), then any injected request headers.
The route configuration builder must always emit `validate_clusters=true` as its final line. This is unconditional and ensures the proxy cannot reference non-existent clusters.
In the SNI listener builder, filter chain entries (sni lines) must be emitted in ascending alphabetical order by server name, regardless of the order the matches appear in the input array.

The SNI listener output must include the line `tls_inspector=true` before any sni match lines. This signals that a TLS-inspector listener filter is attached so the requested server name can be detected.

When access logging is enabled in the connection manager builder, the output must include both `access_log=true` and `access_log_path=/dev/stdout`. When disabled, only `access_log=false` is emitted and no path line appears.

When the settings map is empty (no keys provided), the parser must default `enable_service_access_logging` to `true`. The recognized key in the input map is `enable-service-access-logging` (hyphenated), but the output field name uses underscores: `enable_service_access_logging`.

When the settings map contains a non-boolean string value for `enable-service-access-logging`, the output must be exactly two lines: `error=invalid_boolean` followed by `key=enable-service-access-logging`. No stack traces or runtime exceptions should surface.

For all listener types (plain HTTP, HTTPS, SNI), the listener name is always derived as `listener_<port>` where `<port>` is the bound port number. For example port 8080 produces `listener=listener_8080`.

The cluster builder must always emit the `http2` field. When HTTP/2 is not requested the output is `http2=false` — the field is never omitted. Only when HTTP/2 is requested is `http2=true` emitted, along with explicit upstream protocol options at the proxy level.

The codebase must NOT be a single monolithic file. Builders must be separated by resource type (endpoint/cluster, route, virtual host, listener/TLS, connection manager), the settings parser must be independent, and the execution adapter (stdin/stdout/JSON) must be physically separate from the core builders.

Certificate chain and private key values must be embedded and output verbatim — exactly as supplied in the input, byte-for-byte. No encoding, transformation, or truncation is applied. This applies to both the single-cert HTTPS listener and each SNI match entry.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'lb_endpoint', 'cluster', 'weighted_cluster', 'route', 'virtual_host', 'connection_manager', 'route_config', 'http_listener', 'https_listener', 'sni_listener', 'parse_config'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The IPv4-compat flag (`ipv4_compat=true`) that must always be hardcoded as true in the lb_endpoint builder, as specified in Feature 1 of the PRD

context_pointers.vague_reference: the websocket situation we always forget about
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The unconditional `upgrade type=websocket enabled=true` line that every forwarding route must emit, defined in Feature 4 of the PRD
The HTTP Connection Manager builder (Feature 6), which sets stat_prefix=ingress_http, codec_type=AUTO, and optional access logging to /dev/stdout

The parse_config operation (Feature 9) that reads a string-keyed map with key `enable-service-access-logging` and produces a typed settings object with default true and normalized error on invalid boolean

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect from my end — you'll want to dig into the existing proxy config patterns in the codebase or check with the platform team. The general principle is that safe defaults should always be baked in so operators can't accidentally ship a broken config.

feature1_lb_endpoint.json.description: Builds a single load-balancer endpoint from an upstream IP address and port. The endpoint is rendered as a TCP socket address that downstream proxies use to reach the backend. IPv4-compat is always enabled.

feature2_cluster.json.description: Builds an upstream cluster from a name, a connect timeout, a list of endpoints, an HTTP/2 toggle, and a discovery type. When HTTP/2 is requested the cluster carries explicit HTTP/2 upstream protocol options; otherwise no protocol options are attached. The endpoints are echoed back under the cluster's load assignment.

cases.expected_output:
cluster=myTestCluster_12345
connect_timeout_seconds=5
discovery_type=STATIC
http2=true
endpoint address=127.0.0.1 port=1234
endpoint address=127.0.0.2 port=1234

cluster=myTestCluster_12345
connect_timeout_seconds=5
discovery_type=STATIC
http2=false
endpoint address=127.0.0.1 port=1234
endpoint address=127.0.0.2 port=1234

feature3_weighted_cluster.json.description: Builds a weighted cluster entry for traffic splitting: a target cluster name, an integer traffic weight, and an optional set of request headers to inject. Each header is emitted with append disabled, meaning the header is set (overwritten) rather than appended. Header lines are emitted in ascending key order.

cases.expected_output:
cluster=test
weight=50
header key=foo value=bar append=false

cluster=split-a
weight=75
header key=baz value=lol append=false
header key=foo value=bar append=false

feature4_route.json.description: Builds a routing rule that matches a request path prefix and optional exact header matches, then forwards to weighted clusters. Every forwarding route enables websocket upgrades and carries a request timeout. An optional host-rewrite literal overrides the upstream Host header. Optional request headers are injected with append disabled and emitted in ascending key order.

cases.expected_output:
route=testRoute_12345
path_prefix=/my_route
header_match name=myHeader exact=strict
upgrade type=websocket enabled=true
timeout_seconds=0

route=testRoute_12345
path_prefix=/my_route
upgrade type=websocket enabled=true
timeout_seconds=0
host_rewrite=test.host

feature5_1_virtual_host_plain.json.description: Builds a plain virtual host from a name, a list of matching domains, and a list of route names. No external-authorization per-filter configuration is attached.

feature5_2_virtual_host_ext_authz.json.description: Builds a virtual host that additionally carries an external-authorization per-route filter configuration. The name, domains, and routes are preserved exactly as for a plain virtual host, but the external-authorization filter configuration is now present.

feature6_connection_manager.json.description: Builds an HTTP connection manager that forwards routing decisions to a named route configuration fetched dynamically. The codec is auto-detected and a fixed statistics prefix is used. When access logging is enabled, request logs are written to the standard output path; when disabled, no access log is attached.

feature7_route_config.json.description: Builds a route configuration from a name and a list of virtual host names. Cluster validation is always enabled so the configuration cannot reference non-existent clusters.

feature8_1_http_listener.json.description: Builds a plain HTTP listener bound to all interfaces on a given port. The listener name is derived from the port. No TLS transport is configured.

feature8_2_https_listener.json.description: Builds an HTTPS listener bound to all interfaces on a given port, backed by a single TLS filter chain built from a certificate chain and a private key. The certificate chain and private key embedded in the listener round-trip back to the original input bytes.

cases.expected_output:
listener=listener_8081
protocol=TCP
address=0.0.0.0
port=8081
tls=true
certificate_chain=some_certificate_chain
private_key=some_private_key

feature8_3_sni_listener.json.description: Builds an HTTPS listener that selects a TLS filter chain by the requested server name (SNI). For each SNI match a dedicated filter chain is created carrying that match's host names, certificate chain, and private key. A TLS-inspector listener filter is attached so the requested server name can be detected. SNI lines are emitted in ascending server-name order, and each match's certificate/key round-trips back to its input bytes.

cases.expected_output:
listener=listener_8443
protocol=TCP
address=0.0.0.0
port=8443
tls_inspector=true
sni server_names=another_host.com certificate_chain=cert2 private_key=key2
sni server_names=some_host.com certificate_chain=cert1 private_key=key1

feature9_parse_config.json.description: Parses a string-keyed configuration map into the gateway settings object. The only recognized key toggles service access logging and must hold a boolean string. When the key is absent the setting defaults to enabled. When the key holds a non-boolean string the parse fails with a normalized invalid-boolean error naming the offending key.
